finops_results.policy_name: [CloudFront.3] CloudFront distributions should require encryption in transit
finops_results.policy_message: Consider requiring HTTPS or redirecting HTTP to HTTPS on CloudFront distributions to protect data in transit. Unencrypted HTTP connections expose data to interception and man-in-the-middle attacks. Corresponds to [AWS Security Hub CloudFront.3](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/cloudfront-controls.html#cloudfront-3).

finops_results.policy_name: CloudFront - distributions should have WAF enabled
finops_results.policy_message: CloudFront distributions should have WAF enabled to help protect against malicious attacks. AWS WAF is a web application firewall that lets you monitor HTTP(S) requests and control access to your content. Corresponds to [AWS Security Hub CloudFront.6](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/cloudfront-controls.html#cloudfront-6).

issues.description: Consider attaching a WAF web ACL to this CloudFront distribution. [CloudFront.6] Set web_acl_id to the ARN of an AWS WAF web ACL to help protect against malicious attacks.
Consider attaching a WAF web ACL to this CloudFront distribution. [CloudFront.6] Set web_acl_id to the ARN of an AWS WAF web ACL to help protect against malicious attacks.
Consider attaching a WAF web ACL to this CloudFront distribution. [CloudFront.6] Set web_acl_id to the ARN of an AWS WAF web ACL to help protect against malicious attacks.

finops_results.policy_name: S3 - consider deleting incomplete multi-part uploads
finops_results.policy_message: When S3 uploads fail, the incomplete multi-part data still lives in S3 and costs money even though it's not a usable S3 object. You can avoid paying for that by setting the number of days after which these are deleted. We suggest 7 days.

finops_results.policy_name: S3 - consider deleting or moving non-current object versions to a cheaper storage class
finops_results.policy_message: S3 can store multiple versions of the same object. However, most of the time, you only need the current version. So you could set the number of days after which non-current objects are deleted. Ensure that you do not need non-current object versions first.

issues.description: Add a `aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration` resource to define a `noncurrent_version_expiration` rule
Add a `aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration` resource to define a `noncurrent_version_expiration` rule
Add a `rule` which specifies a `noncurrent_version_expiration` configuration

finops_results.policy_name: [EC2.2] VPC default security groups should not allow inbound or outbound traffic
finops_results.policy_message: Consider ensuring default security groups do not allow any inbound or outbound traffic. AWS recommends that you create security groups for specific resources instead of using the default security group. Corresponds to [AWS Security Hub EC2.2](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/ec2-controls.html#ec2-2).

finops_results.policy_name: [EC2.9, EC2.25] Amazon EC2 and launch templates should not associate a public IPv4 address
finops_results.policy_message: EC2 instances and launch templates should not have a public IPv4 address to reduce attack surface and prevent direct internet exposure. Use NAT gateways for outbound connectivity and AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for secure instance access. Corresponds to AWS Security Hub control EC2.9 and EC2.25.

issues.description: Consider disabling `associate_public_ip_address`. Set `associate_public_ip_address = false` and use NAT gateways for outbound connectivity.
Consider disabling `associate_public_ip_address`. Set `associate_public_ip_address = false` and use NAT gateways for outbound connectivity.
Consider disabling `associate_public_ip_address`. Set `associate_public_ip_address = false` and use NAT gateways for outbound connectivity.
Consider disabling `associate_public_ip_address` in `network_interfaces`. Set `associate_public_ip_address = false` and use NAT gateways for outbound connectivity.
Consider disabling `associate_public_ip_address`. Set `associate_public_ip_address = false` and use NAT gateways for outbound connectivity.

finops_results.policy_name: EC2 - require Instance Metadata Service Version 2 (IMDSv2)
finops_results.policy_message: Consider using IMDSv2 for EC2 instances to provide enhanced security protections against multiple vulnerability classes. Corresponds to AWS Security Hub control EC2.8.

finops_results.policy_name: [ELB.1] Application Load Balancer should be configured to redirect all HTTP requests to HTTPS
finops_results.policy_message: Consider configuring Application Load Balancer to redirect all HTTP requests to HTTPS to encrypt data in transit. Corresponds to [AWS Security Hub ELB.1](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/elb-controls.html#elb-1).

finops_results.policy_name: S3 - consider using Intelligent-Tiering or adding a lifecycle policy
finops_results.policy_message: Use a lifecycle policy to reduce object storage costs. You can reduce storage costs by defining policies that remove or move objects to a less expensive S3 storage class. For example, a bucket storing 1TB of data in a standard storage class would have a monthly cost of $23. If the same data was moved to Glacier Flexible Retrieval storage class it would have a monthly cost of just $3.60. That's a 84% cost saving.

issues.description: Add a `aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration` resource to define `aws_s3_bucket` object lifecycles
Add a `aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration` resource to define `aws_s3_bucket` object lifecycles
Add a `aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration` resource to define `aws_s3_bucket` object lifecycles
Add a `aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration` resource to define `aws_s3_bucket` object lifecycles
Add a `aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration` resource to define `aws_s3_bucket` object lifecycles
Add a `aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration` resource to define `aws_s3_bucket` object lifecycles

finops_results.policy_name: EBS - consider upgrading gp2 volumes to gp3
finops_results.policy_message: gp3 volumes are the latest generation of general-purpose SSD-based EBS volumes that enable you to provision performance independent of storage capacity, while providing up to **20% lower price per GB** than existing gp2 volumes. With gp3 volumes, you can scale IOPS (input/output operations per second) and throughput without needing to provision additional block storage capacity. This means you only pay for the storage you need.

finops_results.policy_name: EBS - consider updating io1/io2 volumes to gp3
finops_results.policy_message: io1 and io2 volume types that need less than 16K IOPS, and less than 1000 MB/s Throughput, and don’t use Multi-Attach should be switched to gp3 as that is **at least 2X cheaper**. For example, a gp3 volume with 16TB storage, 16K IOPS and 1000 MB/s Throughput costs $1.4K/month vs $3K/month on io1 or io2.

finops_results.policy_name: KMS - consider enabling automatic key rotation
finops_results.policy_message: Consider enabling automatic key rotation for AWS KMS keys to reduce the potential impact of a compromised key. Rotating encryption keys helps ensure that data encrypted with a new key cannot be accessed with a previous key that might have been exposed. Corresponds to [AWS Security Hub KMS.4](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/kms-controls.html#kms-4)

finops_results.policy_name: VPC - consider avoiding multiple NAT gateways for a single subnet
finops_results.policy_message: Each NAT gateway costs around $420/year so when possible, reuse existing gateways to avoid unnecessary costs.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS Cluster - consider enabling CloudWatch log exports
finops_results.policy_message: Consider enabling CloudWatch log exports for RDS clusters and instances to enable monitoring and auditing capabilities. This helps with troubleshooting, performance analysis, and compliance requirements.

issues.description: Consider enabling CloudWatch log exports for this RDS cluster. Set `enabled_cloudwatch_logs_exports` to include the right log types (see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-9) to enable monitoring and auditing capabilities.

Consider enabling CloudWatch log exports for this RDS instance. Set `enabled_cloudwatch_logs_exports` to include the right log types (see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-9) to enable monitoring and auditing capabilities.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS Cluster - should copy tags to snapshots
finops_results.policy_message: Consider configuring RDS DB instances and clusters to copy tags to snapshots to maintain consistent resource tagging and support asset tracking. Corresponds to AWS Security Hub [RDS.16](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-16) (Aurora clusters) and [RDS.17](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-17) (DB instances).

issues.description: Set `copy_tags_to_snapshot` = true
Set `copy_tags_to_snapshot` = true
Set `copy_tags_to_snapshot` = true
Set `copy_tags_to_snapshot` = true

finops_results.policy_name: [RDS.3] RDS DB instances should have encryption at-rest enabled
finops_results.policy_message:
RDS DB instances and clusters should have encryption at rest enabled to protect sensitive data and meet compliance requirements. Corresponds to [AWS Security Hub RDS.3](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-3).

NOTE: Encryption at rest cannot be enabled on existing unencrypted databases. To enable encryption on an existing database, you must create a snapshot, create an encrypted copy of the snapshot, restore from the encrypted snapshot to a new database instance, and migrate applications. This process requires downtime and careful planning.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider upgrading gp2 storage type to gp3
finops_results.policy_message: gp3 is the latest generation storage type with general-purpose SSDs that enable you to provision performance independent of storage capacity, while providing up to **20% lower price per GB** than the existing gp2 storage type. With gp3, you can scale IOPS (input/output operations per second) and throughput without needing to provision additional block storage capacity. This means you only pay for the storage you need.

finops_results.policy_name: EKS - consider upgrading version to avoid extended support costs
finops_results.policy_message: Consider upgrading your EKS cluster version to avoid significant additional costs associated with extended support. For example, a single EKS cluster running on Kubernetes version 1.25 incurs a monthly management cost of approximately $73 (without extended support). Extended support increases these costs by 600%, bringing the total monthly cost to ~$438. Upgrading your cluster version would eliminate these extra expenses. See [AWS docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/kubernetes-versions.html) for supported versions.

issues.description: Upgrade `version` to avoid extended support costs which come into effect in March 2026.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider upgrading version to avoid extended support costs
finops_results.policy_message: Consider upgrading your RDS database engine version to avoid extended support costs. Extended support costs can add a significant additional cost to your database. For example, a Single A-Z RDS database using MySQL 5.7 and an instance class db.t3.large has a monthly cost of ~$101 (considering a 20GB SSD). Extended support for MySQL 5.7, which started in March 2024, adds an additional $146 of operating costs – a 140% increase that could be saved by upgrading the engine version. See [AWS docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/PostgreSQLReleaseNotes/postgresql-release-calendar.html) for supported versions.

issues.description: Upgrade `engine_version` from `13.15` to a minimum supported `postgres` version to avoid extended support costs which come into effect on March 2026.

finops_results.policy_name: ElastiCache - consider using latest generation nodes for t family nodes
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to the latest generation of Amazon ElastiCache nodes in the T family for improved CPU performance and better price efficiency compared to earlier generations.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider using latest generation instances for t family instances
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to the latest generation of Amazon RDS instances in the T family for improved CPU performance while maintaining cost efficiency.

finops_results.policy_name: Cloudwatch - consider using a retention policy to reduce storage costs
finops_results.policy_message: Use a retention policy to avoid retaining logs indefinitely and accumulating storage costs. For example, if an application generates 5GB of logs daily, in a year without retention, you'd store 1.8TB of logs. In the AWS US East (N. Virginia) region, where storage costs $0.03 per GB per month, you'll incur a cumulative yearly cost of ~$802. This cost will continually grow as more logs are added.

issues.description: Update `retention_in_days` to a value greater than `0`

finops_results.policy_name: ECR - consider using a lifecycle policy
finops_results.policy_message: A lifecycle policy should be defined for Amazon ECR repositories to automate the management of image retention, optimize storage costs, improve performance, and remove images that are no longer needed. For example, storing 1TB of images in ECR costs $100/month. Reducing this to 100GB by removing unused images greater than 30 days old reduces that to $4/month. That's a 96% cost saving.

finops_results.policy_name: EC2 - consider using Graviton instances
finops_results.policy_message: Graviton instances are around 20% cheaper than equivalent x86 instances.

issues.description: Switch `instance_types[0]` from `t3.large` to `t4g.large`
Switch `instance_types[0]` from `t3.large` to `t4g.large`
Switch `instance_types[0]` from `t3.large` to `t4g.large`
Switch `instance_type` from `t3.medium` to `t4g.medium`

finops_results.policy_name: Lambda - consider using Graviton
finops_results.policy_message: Graviton lambda functions are 20% cheaper than x86 equivalents with up to 19% improved performance. If you do not use x86 binary dependencies, setting lambda functions to use ARM64 can result in significant savings and improved performance. See [AWS docs](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/aws-lambda-functions-powered-by-aws-graviton2-processor-run-your-functions-on-arm-and-get-up-to-34-better-price-performance/) for details.

issues.description: Set `architectures` to include `arm64`
Set `architectures` to include `arm64`

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider using Graviton instances
finops_results.policy_message: Graviton instances are around 20% cheaper than equivalent x86 instances.

finops_results.policy_name: EC2 - consider using latest generation instances for r family instances
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to the latest generation of Amazon EC2 instances in the R family for optimized memory performance, improved price per GiB of RAM, and additional features like newer processors, default EBS optimization, and Enhanced Networking support, all at a more cost-effective price compared to previous generations.

finops_results.policy_name: EC2 - consider using latest generation instances for t family instances
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to Amazon EC2 t3 instances for improved Intel Xeon Processors, AWS Nitro System and EBS optimization by default, all at lower price point than previous generations. For example a `t2.medium` instance with 4 GiB of memory and 2 vCPUs (for a 4h 48m burst) has a monthly cost of $33. Whereas a `t3.medium` instance with equivalent machine specs costs just $30. That's a 10% cost saving for a more performant machine.

finops_results.policy_name: DynamoDB - consider using provisioned tables rather than on-demand
finops_results.policy_message: You should use Provisioned DynamoDB rather than On-Demand if your application has predictable traffic patterns and high, steady throughput requirements because it allows for cost optimization by reserving capacity in advance. On-Demand is more suitable for unpredictable workloads. For example, a 100GB table with 5000 Read Capacity Units (RCUs) and 2000 Write Capacity Units (WCUs) with 50m reads and 20m writes a day would cost $1,400/month provisioned. The same table configured as On-Demand would cost $2,600/month - a saving of 46% by using provisioned capacity.

issues.description: Change the billing mode of the DynamoDB table to PROVISIONED
Change the billing mode of the DynamoDB table to PROVISIONED
Change the billing mode of the DynamoDB table to PROVISIONED

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider using single-AZ databases in non-production projects
finops_results.policy_message: Limit your RDS databases to a single availability zone in non-prod environments to reduce costs by around half. For example, a db.m4.large utilizing multi-AZ costs ~$310/month, whereas single-AZ costs ~$166/month.

finops_results.policy_name: VPC - consider avoiding multiple VPC endpoints for the same service
finops_results.policy_message: Each VPC endpoint costs around $80/year so when possible, reuse existing endpoints to avoid unnecessary costs.

tagging_results.message: This is a test tagging policy to validate scheduled data ingestion of cloud costs via AWS CE (TRA-470).

finops_results.policy_name: EBS - consider upgrading gp2 volumes to gp3
finops_results.policy_message: gp3 volumes are the latest generation of general-purpose SSD-based EBS volumes that enable you to provision performance independent of storage capacity, while providing up to **20% lower price per GB** than existing gp2 volumes. With gp3 volumes, you can scale IOPS (input/output operations per second) and throughput without needing to provision additional block storage capacity. This means you only pay for the storage you need.

finops_results.policy_name: EBS - consider updating io1/io2 volumes to gp3
finops_results.policy_message: io1 and io2 volume types that need less than 16K IOPS, and less than 1000 MB/s Throughput, and don’t use Multi-Attach should be switched to gp3 as that is **at least 2X cheaper**. For example, a gp3 volume with 16TB storage, 16K IOPS and 1000 MB/s Throughput costs $1.4K/month vs $3K/month on io1 or io2.

finops_results.policy_name: KMS - consider enabling automatic key rotation
finops_results.policy_message: Consider enabling automatic key rotation for AWS KMS keys to reduce the potential impact of a compromised key. Rotating encryption keys helps ensure that data encrypted with a new key cannot be accessed with a previous key that might have been exposed. Corresponds to [AWS Security Hub KMS.4](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/kms-controls.html#kms-4)

finops_results.policy_name: VPC - consider avoiding multiple NAT gateways for a single subnet
finops_results.policy_message: Each NAT gateway costs around $420/year so when possible, reuse existing gateways to avoid unnecessary costs.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS Cluster - consider enabling CloudWatch log exports
finops_results.policy_message: Consider enabling CloudWatch log exports for RDS clusters and instances to enable monitoring and auditing capabilities. This helps with troubleshooting, performance analysis, and compliance requirements.

issues.description: Consider enabling CloudWatch log exports for this RDS cluster. Set `enabled_cloudwatch_logs_exports` to include the right log types (see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-9) to enable monitoring and auditing capabilities.

Consider enabling CloudWatch log exports for this RDS cluster. Set `enabled_cloudwatch_logs_exports` to include the right log types (see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-9) to enable monitoring and auditing capabilities.

Consider enabling CloudWatch log exports for this RDS instance. Set `enabled_cloudwatch_logs_exports` to include the right log types (see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-9) to enable monitoring and auditing capabilities.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS Cluster - should copy tags to snapshots
finops_results.policy_message: Consider configuring RDS DB instances and clusters to copy tags to snapshots to maintain consistent resource tagging and support asset tracking. Corresponds to AWS Security Hub [RDS.16](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-16) (Aurora clusters) and [RDS.17](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-17) (DB instances).

issues.description: Set `copy_tags_to_snapshot` = true
Set `copy_tags_to_snapshot` = true

finops_results.policy_name: [RDS.3] RDS DB instances should have encryption at-rest enabled
finops_results.policy_message:
RDS DB instances and clusters should have encryption at rest enabled to protect sensitive data and meet compliance requirements. Corresponds to [AWS Security Hub RDS.3](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/securityhub/latest/userguide/rds-controls.html#rds-3).

NOTE: Encryption at rest cannot be enabled on existing unencrypted databases. To enable encryption on an existing database, you must create a snapshot, create an encrypted copy of the snapshot, restore from the encrypted snapshot to a new database instance, and migrate applications. This process requires downtime and careful planning.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider upgrading gp2 storage type to gp3
finops_results.policy_message: gp3 is the latest generation storage type with general-purpose SSDs that enable you to provision performance independent of storage capacity, while providing up to **20% lower price per GB** than the existing gp2 storage type. With gp3, you can scale IOPS (input/output operations per second) and throughput without needing to provision additional block storage capacity. This means you only pay for the storage you need.

finops_results.policy_name: EKS - consider upgrading version to avoid extended support costs
finops_results.policy_message: Consider upgrading your EKS cluster version to avoid significant additional costs associated with extended support. For example, a single EKS cluster running on Kubernetes version 1.25 incurs a monthly management cost of approximately $73 (without extended support). Extended support increases these costs by 600%, bringing the total monthly cost to ~$438. Upgrading your cluster version would eliminate these extra expenses. See [AWS docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/kubernetes-versions.html) for supported versions.

issues.description: Upgrade `version` to avoid extended support costs which come into effect in March 2026.

finops_results.policy_name: ElastiCache - consider using latest generation node for m family nodes
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to the latest generation of Amazon ElastiCache nodes in the M family for enhanced CPU performance and improved networking bandwidth, offering better value compared to previous generations.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider using latest generation instances for r family instances
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to the latest generation of Amazon RDS instances in the R family for enhanced CPU performance, improved memory capacity, and better overall efficiency compared to previous generations.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider upgrading version to avoid extended support costs
finops_results.policy_message: Consider upgrading your RDS database engine version to avoid extended support costs. Extended support costs can add a significant additional cost to your database. For example, a Single A-Z RDS database using MySQL 5.7 and an instance class db.t3.large has a monthly cost of ~$101 (considering a 20GB SSD). Extended support for MySQL 5.7, which started in March 2024, adds an additional $146 of operating costs – a 140% increase that could be saved by upgrading the engine version. See [AWS docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonRDS/latest/PostgreSQLReleaseNotes/postgresql-release-calendar.html) for supported versions.

issues.description: Upgrade `engine_version` from `13.15` to a minimum supported `postgres` version to avoid extended support costs which come into effect on March 2026.

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider using latest generation instances for t family instances
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to the latest generation of Amazon RDS instances in the T family for improved CPU performance while maintaining cost efficiency.

finops_results.policy_name: Cloudwatch - consider using a retention policy to reduce storage costs
finops_results.policy_message: Use a retention policy to avoid retaining logs indefinitely and accumulating storage costs. For example, if an application generates 5GB of logs daily, in a year without retention, you'd store 1.8TB of logs. In the AWS US East (N. Virginia) region, where storage costs $0.03 per GB per month, you'll incur a cumulative yearly cost of ~$802. This cost will continually grow as more logs are added.

issues.description: Update `retention_in_days` to a value greater than `0`

finops_results.policy_name: ECR - consider using a lifecycle policy
finops_results.policy_message: A lifecycle policy should be defined for Amazon ECR repositories to automate the management of image retention, optimize storage costs, improve performance, and remove images that are no longer needed. For example, storing 1TB of images in ECR costs $100/month. Reducing this to 100GB by removing unused images greater than 30 days old reduces that to $4/month. That's a 96% cost saving.

issues.description: Add a `aws_ecr_lifecycle_policy` resource to define a lifecycle policy for the repository
Add a `aws_ecr_lifecycle_policy` resource to define a lifecycle policy for the repository

finops_results.policy_name: EC2 - consider using Graviton instances
finops_results.policy_message: Graviton instances are around 20% cheaper than equivalent x86 instances.

issues.description: Switch `instance_types[0]` from `r7i.large` to `r7g.large`
Switch `instance_types[0]` from `t3.large` to `t4g.large`
Switch `instance_types[0]` from `r7i.xlarge` to `r7g.xlarge`

finops_results.policy_name: Lambda - consider using Graviton
finops_results.policy_message: Graviton lambda functions are 20% cheaper than x86 equivalents with up to 19% improved performance. If you do not use x86 binary dependencies, setting lambda functions to use ARM64 can result in significant savings and improved performance. See [AWS docs](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/aws-lambda-functions-powered-by-aws-graviton2-processor-run-your-functions-on-arm-and-get-up-to-34-better-price-performance/) for details.

issues.description: Set `architectures` to include `arm64`
Set `architectures` to include `arm64`

finops_results.policy_name: RDS - consider using Graviton instances
finops_results.policy_message: Graviton instances are around 20% cheaper than equivalent x86 instances.

finops_results.policy_name: EC2 - consider using latest generation instances for m family instances
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to the latest generation of Amazon EC2 instances in the M family for enhanced CPU performance, increased memory capacity, and improved networking bandwidth, all at a more cost-effective price compared to previous generations.

finops_results.policy_name: EC2 - consider using latest generation instances for r family instances
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to the latest generation of Amazon EC2 instances in the R family for optimized memory performance, improved price per GiB of RAM, and additional features like newer processors, default EBS optimization, and Enhanced Networking support, all at a more cost-effective price compared to previous generations.

finops_results.policy_name: EC2 - consider using latest generation instances for t family instances
finops_results.policy_message: Upgrade to Amazon EC2 t3 instances for improved Intel Xeon Processors, AWS Nitro System and EBS optimization by default, all at lower price point than previous generations. For example a `t2.medium` instance with 4 GiB of memory and 2 vCPUs (for a 4h 48m burst) has a monthly cost of $33. Whereas a `t3.medium` instance with equivalent machine specs costs just $30. That's a 10% cost saving for a more performant machine.

finops_results.policy_name: DynamoDB - consider using provisioned tables rather than on-demand
finops_results.policy_message: You should use Provisioned DynamoDB rather than On-Demand if your application has predictable traffic patterns and high, steady throughput requirements because it allows for cost optimization by reserving capacity in advance. On-Demand is more suitable for unpredictable workloads. For example, a 100GB table with 5000 Read Capacity Units (RCUs) and 2000 Write Capacity Units (WCUs) with 50m reads and 20m writes a day would cost $1,400/month provisioned. The same table configured as On-Demand would cost $2,600/month - a saving of 46% by using provisioned capacity.

issues.description: Change the billing mode of the DynamoDB table to PROVISIONED
Change the billing mode of the DynamoDB table to PROVISIONED
Change the billing mode of the DynamoDB table to PROVISIONED

finops_results.policy_name: VPC - consider avoiding multiple VPC endpoints for the same service
finops_results.policy_message: Each VPC endpoint costs around $80/year so when possible, reuse existing endpoints to avoid unnecessary costs.

finops_results.policy_name: S3 - consider deleting incomplete multi-part uploads
finops_results.policy_message: When S3 uploads fail, the incomplete multi-part data still lives in S3 and costs money even though it's not a usable S3 object. You can avoid paying for that by setting the number of days after which these are deleted. We suggest 7 days.

finops_results.policy_name: S3 - consider deleting or moving non-current object versions to a cheaper storage class
finops_results.policy_message: S3 can store multiple versions of the same object. However, most of the time, you only need the current version. So you could set the number of days after which non-current objects are deleted. Ensure that you do not need non-current object versions first.

finops_results.policy_name: S3 - consider using Intelligent-Tiering or adding a lifecycle policy
finops_results.policy_message: Use a lifecycle policy to reduce object storage costs. You can reduce storage costs by defining policies that remove or move objects to a less expensive S3 storage class. For example, a bucket storing 1TB of data in a standard storage class would have a monthly cost of $23. If the same data was moved to Glacier Flexible Retrieval storage class it would have a monthly cost of just $3.60. That's a 84% cost saving.

finops_results.policy_name: Cloudwatch - consider using a retention policy to reduce storage costs
finops_results.policy_message: Use a retention policy to avoid retaining logs indefinitely and accumulating storage costs. For example, if an application generates 5GB of logs daily, in a year without retention, you'd store 1.8TB of logs. In the AWS US East (N. Virginia) region, where storage costs $0.03 per GB per month, you'll incur a cumulative yearly cost of ~$802. This cost will continually grow as more logs are added.

finops_results.policy_name: Lambda - consider using Graviton
finops_results.policy_message: Graviton lambda functions are 20% cheaper than x86 equivalents with up to 19% improved performance. If you do not use x86 binary dependencies, setting lambda functions to use ARM64 can result in significant savings and improved performance. See [AWS docs](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws/aws-lambda-functions-powered-by-aws-graviton2-processor-run-your-functions-on-arm-and-get-up-to-34-better-price-performance/) for details.
